title: 山核桃大街谋杀案
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\121-山核桃大街谋杀案-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Hercule Poirot arranges an afternoon tea with Miss Lemon’s sister, Mrs. Haberdashery (also referred to as Mrs. Hubbard/Mrs. Harbottle), to discuss a series of unusual thefts. Mrs. Haberdashery provides a notebook containing a list of missing items, which includes an evening dress shoe, artificial jewelry, a powder box, lipstick, a stethoscope, earrings, a cigarette lighter, old flannel pants, a light bulb, a box of chocolates, a torn scarf, a canvas backpack, boric acid powder, and Epsom salt. Notably, a diamond ring—belonging to Patricia Lean—was previously found in a soup bowl belonging to Valerie Hobhouse.

Poirot finds the random nature of the list intriguing, comparing it to a memory game called "The Three-Headed Woman." He notes that while many items are of little value, the diamond ring and the stethoscope are significant. Mrs. Haberdashery explains that the ring was Patricia Lean's mother's engagement ring; its return in Valerie Hobhouse's soup dish was initially dismissed as a prank. 

Poirot begins to analyze the victims and the items. He learns that Patricia Lean is a quiet, plain, and respectable girl studying archaeology who is not wealthy and has recently quit smoking. Valerie Hobhouse is described as a clever, dark-skinned woman with a sharp tongue who works at a beauty salon called "Sabrina Goddess." Poirot observes that while some items might tempt a vain, cash-strapped girl, the stethoscope—which belongs to a pleasant young man named Mr. Bateson—suggests a thief who knows how to pawn professional equipment.
